name: Oman
tagline: From the copper of Magan and the frankincense roads to a seafaring empire that reached Zanzibar, reborn as a modern sultanate.
summary: Oman occupies the southeastern corner of the Arabian Peninsula, a land of mountains, desert and a long Indian Ocean coast that has made it one of the oldest seafaring and trading societies on Earth. In antiquity it was the copper-rich land the Sumerians called Magan and the heartland of the frankincense trade that linked southern Arabia to the Mediterranean. It adopted Islam in the Prophet's lifetime and became the world's principal home of the Ibadi school, governed for centuries by elected imams. Under the Al Bu Said dynasty, founded in 1744, Oman built a maritime empire that stretched to Zanzibar and the East African coast, before a long decline, growing British influence, and the 1970 accession of Sultan Qaboos bin Said opened the country's modern renaissance.

founding.label: Founding of the Al Bu Said dynasty
founding.detail: The modern Omani state dates to the rise of Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi, governor of Suhar, who drove out the occupying Persians and was elected imam around 1744-1749, founding the Al Bu Said dynasty that still rules Oman today. Its far deeper roots reach back to the ancient land of Magan, prized by Mesopotamia for its copper, and to the early adoption of Islam and the Ibadi imamate in the 8th century, which gave Oman its distinctive religious and political identity.

quickFacts.label: National Day
quickFacts.value: 18 November, marking the birthday of the late Sultan Qaboos bin Said

quickFacts.label: Ruling dynasty
quickFacts.value: Al Bu Said, ruling since around 1744

quickFacts.label: Predominant religion
quickFacts.value: Islam; Oman is the principal home of the Ibadi school

quickFacts.label: Ancient identity
quickFacts.value: The copper-land of Magan and a heart of the frankincense trade

quickFacts.label: Current head of state
quickFacts.value: Sultan Haitham bin Tariq, acceded January 2020

eras.title: Magan, Copper and the Land of Frankincense
eras.standfirst: Long before Islam, Oman was the copper-rich land the Sumerians called Magan and a heartland of the frankincense trade that perfumed the temples of the ancient world.
body: From the third millennium BCE the mountains of Oman supplied one of the ancient Near East's most coveted commodities: copper. Sumerian cuneiform texts name a land called Magan as the source of copper and diorite shipped to Mesopotamia, and the smelting of ore in the Omani interior reached an almost proto-industrial scale. Magan's shipwrights and merchants tied Oman into a maritime network that reached the Indus valley, Mesopotamia and Iran, making the region a hub of Bronze Age exchange.

The most spectacular trace of this era survives in the interior at Bat, Al-Khutm and Al-Ayn, inscribed by UNESCO in 1988. Dated to the third millennium BCE, the site preserves monumental stone towers and the world's most complete collection of beehive-shaped dry-stone tombs, evidence of organized communities linked by trade to Mesopotamia and the Indus. The towers, with their circular outer walls and inner compartments, and the necropolises that surround them, map a sophisticated society at the dawn of Oman's recorded history.

In the south, the Dhofar region became the cradle of the frankincense trade, the source of a fragrant resin tapped from the Boswellia sacra tree and prized across antiquity for ritual, medicine and luxury. The frankincense groves of Wadi Dawkah and the caravan and port sites of Khor Rori, Al-Baleed and Shisr (sometimes identified with Ubar), together inscribed by UNESCO in 2000 as the Land of Frankincense, illustrate a trade that flourished for centuries and carried Arabian incense to the Mediterranean world.

events.title: Magan supplies copper to Mesopotamia
events.summary: Sumerian texts record a land called Magan, identified with Oman, as a source of copper and diorite carried by sea to the city-states of Mesopotamia.

events.title: Beehive tombs and towers at Bat
events.summary: The Bronze Age communities of Bat, Al-Khutm and Al-Ayn raised monumental stone towers and dry-stone beehive tombs, later inscribed by UNESCO in 1988 as the most complete such collection in the world.

events.title: The frankincense trade flourishes in Dhofar
events.summary: The resin of the Boswellia sacra tree, harvested in Dhofar, became one of antiquity's great luxury goods, carried by caravan and ship from southern Arabia to the Mediterranean.

events.title: Khor Rori and the incense ports
events.summary: The port of Khor Rori (Sumhuram) and later Al-Baleed served as gateways for the export of frankincense and goods across the Indian Ocean, anchoring the Land of Frankincense.

pullquote.text: Oman entered written history as Magan, the land whose copper armed Mesopotamia and whose frankincense perfumed the ancient world.

eras.title: Islam and the Ibadi Imamate
eras.standfirst: Oman embraced Islam in the Prophet's lifetime and became the principal home of the Ibadi school, governed by elected imams who fused spiritual and temporal authority.
body: Arab tribes had settled Oman well before Islam, but the new faith arrived early: tradition holds that Omanis accepted Islam during the lifetime of the Prophet Muhammad, around 630 CE. In the generations that followed, Omanis took part in the formation of the Ibadi movement, a distinct and relatively moderate current that emerged from the early Kharijite milieu of Basra. The mountains and the relative isolation of the Omani interior gave the Ibadis a durable refuge, and they won over the leading tribal clans.

By the mid-8th century the Ibadis had built a state of their own under an elected imam who served as both religious and political leader of the community. The first Ibadi imamate is conventionally dated to around 750 CE, when al-Julanda ibn Masud was chosen imam, unifying Oman around the doctrine. The institution of the imamate, with leaders selected for piety and learning rather than by hereditary right, became the defining feature of Omani political life and set the country apart from its neighbors.

For centuries the imamate persisted through cycles of unity and fragmentation, contending with the Abbasid caliphate, regional dynasties and internal rivalries, while the coast and interior often pulled in different directions. Coastal towns prospered on Indian Ocean trade, and Omani sailors ranged across the western Indian Ocean. This enduring religious and maritime identity would shape Oman's response when, in the early 16th century, a new and aggressive sea power appeared off its shores.

events.title: Oman accepts Islam
events.summary: According to tradition, the inhabitants of Oman embraced Islam during the lifetime of the Prophet Muhammad, beginning the country's long Islamic history.

events.title: The first Ibadi imamate
events.summary: Around 750 CE the Ibadis established their first imamate in Oman under al-Julanda ibn Masud, an elected imam uniting temporal and religious authority and giving Oman a distinctive political identity.

events.title: Ibadism becomes Oman's defining school
events.summary: The Ibadi school of Islam took root across the Omani interior, making Oman its principal home and shaping a tradition of imams chosen for learning and piety rather than heredity.

pullquote.text: Where neighbouring lands followed caliphs and kings, Oman chose its imams, blending mountain refuge, maritime reach and a faith all its own.

eras.title: The Portuguese, Hormuz and the Yarubid Recovery
eras.standfirst: Portugal seized Muscat and the Gulf in 1507; more than a century later the Yarubid imams drove them out and turned Oman into an Indian Ocean naval power.
body: In 1507 the Portuguese commander Afonso de Albuquerque, forcing his way into the Indian Ocean, stormed the coastal towns of Oman, including Qalhat and Muscat, imposing tribute and fortifying the harbours. Eight years later Albuquerque captured the island kingdom of Hormuz at the mouth of the Gulf, anchoring a maritime empire that throttled the trade of the region. Muscat, with its deep, defensible bay, became a key Portuguese naval station, dominated by the forts that still overlook the harbour.

Portuguese control was never total, and Omani resistance gathered under a revived imamate. The Yarubid dynasty, founded around 1624 when a member of the Yarub tribe was elected imam, set out to expel the foreigners and restore Omani sovereignty. After decades of pressure, the Yarubids recaptured Muscat in 1650, ending nearly a century and a half of Portuguese occupation of the coast and marking a turning point in the maritime history of the Gulf.

Victory at Muscat launched Oman as an offensive sea power. The Yarubid imams carried the war to the Portuguese across the western Indian Ocean, harrying their possessions on the Indian coast and seizing settlements in the Persian Gulf and along the East African seaboard. This expansion laid the foundations of an Omani Indian Ocean presence that would later reach its height under the Al Bu Said. The dynasty eventually fell into a ruinous succession war that opened the door to a Persian invasion in 1737, plunging Oman into crisis.

events.title: Albuquerque captures Muscat
events.summary: The Portuguese under Afonso de Albuquerque stormed Muscat and other Omani coastal towns, beginning more than a century of Portuguese dominance over the coast.

events.title: Rise of the Yarubid imamate
events.summary: A member of the Yarub tribe was elected imam, founding the Yarubid dynasty that would unite Oman and lead the campaign to expel the Portuguese.

events.title: Expulsion of the Portuguese from Muscat
events.summary: The Yarubid imams recaptured Muscat in 1650, ending Portuguese occupation of the Omani coast and launching Oman as an Indian Ocean naval power.

events.title: Persian invasion amid Yarubid civil war
events.summary: A succession dispute fractured the Yarubid state, and the Persian ruler Nadir Shah's forces invaded and occupied parts of Oman, plunging the country into crisis.

pullquote.text: It took the Omanis a century and a half to drive the Portuguese from Muscat, and once they had, they pursued them across the whole of the Indian Ocean.

eras.title: The Al Bu Said Dynasty and the Empire of Zanzibar
eras.standfirst: Ahmad bin Said expelled the Persians and founded the dynasty that still rules; under Said bin Sultan Oman became an Indian Ocean empire ruling Zanzibar and the East African coast.
body: Out of the chaos of Persian occupation rose Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi, the governor of Suhar, who drove out the invaders and was elected imam around 1744-1749. His leadership reunited the warring tribes, restored Oman's standing in the Gulf, and founded the Al Bu Said dynasty, which has ruled the country ever since. Under Ahmad and his successors the rulers came to be styled sayyids and later sultans, and Omani power and trade revived across the western Indian Ocean.

The empire reached its zenith under Said bin Sultan, who ruled from 1806 to 1856. Building on Oman's old East African connections, he made Zanzibar the commercial hub of the western Indian Ocean, a centre of the trade in cloves, ivory and slaves. So central did the island become that in 1840 Said moved his capital from Muscat to Zanzibar, governing a realm that spanned the Omani coast, the islands and a long stretch of the East African seaboard. In 1833 his government signed a Treaty of Amity and Commerce with the United States, the first such accord between Washington and an Arab Gulf state.

Said's dominions made Oman a genuine maritime power, but they also sowed the seeds of division. On his death in 1856 the realm was split between two of his sons: one took Muscat and Oman, the other Zanzibar, severing the African empire from its Arabian homeland. The separation was formalized soon afterward, and the loss of Zanzibar's revenues would weigh heavily on the Omani sultanate in the decades that followed, as a new external power, Britain, became ever more influential along the coast.

events.title: Ahmad bin Said founds the Al Bu Said dynasty
events.summary: Ahmad bin Said, governor of Suhar, expelled the Persian invaders and was elected imam, founding the Al Bu Said dynasty that still rules Oman.

events.title: Treaty of Amity and Commerce with the United States
events.summary: Said bin Sultan's government concluded a Treaty of Amity and Commerce with the United States, the first bilateral treaty between Washington and an Arab Gulf state.

events.title: Said bin Sultan moves the capital to Zanzibar
events.summary: Said bin Sultan transferred his capital from Muscat to Zanzibar, making the island the commercial heart of his Indian Ocean empire and the chief power in East Africa.

events.title: Death of Said and the split of the empire
events.summary: On Said bin Sultan's death his realm was divided between his sons, one taking Muscat and Oman and the other Zanzibar, severing the African empire from Arabia.

figures.name: Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi
figures.role: Founder and first imam of the Al Bu Said dynasty
figures.blurb: Governor of Suhar who drove out the Persian invaders and was elected imam around 1744-1749, reuniting Oman and founding the dynasty that still rules the country.

figures.name: Said bin Sultan
figures.role: Sultan of Muscat, Oman and Zanzibar
figures.blurb: Ruler from 1806 who built Oman's Indian Ocean empire, made Zanzibar the commercial capital of East Africa, and moved his seat there in 1840.

pullquote.text: Under Said bin Sultan the sultan of Muscat ruled from an island off Africa, and Oman briefly held one of the great commercial empires of the Indian Ocean.

eras.title: Decline, British Influence and Isolation
eras.standfirst: Shorn of Zanzibar and dependent on Britain, Oman turned inward; under Said bin Taimur the country froze into isolation as rebellion smouldered in the interior and Dhofar.
body: The loss of Zanzibar in 1856 stripped Oman of its richest revenues, and the decline of the sailing trade with the rise of steam left the sultanate impoverished and increasingly dependent on Britain, whose subsidies and naval power propped up the rulers in Muscat. Throughout the late 19th and early 20th centuries Oman was effectively split between the British-backed sultans on the coast and a revived Ibadi imamate in the interior, the two reaching an uneasy accommodation that left much of the country beyond Muscat's real control.

From 1932 Sultan Said bin Taimur ruled an Oman frozen in time. Deeply conservative and wary of change, he kept the country isolated and undeveloped, restricting education, travel and modern infrastructure even as oil exploration advanced. He leaned heavily on Britain to hold his realm together, and British forces helped suppress the Jebel Akhdar revolt in the interior in the 1950s, ending the autonomy of the imamate. But discontent deepened, especially in the long-neglected southern province of Dhofar.

In 1965 the Dhofar rebellion erupted, growing from a regional revolt into a Marxist-led insurgency backed from abroad that by 1970 controlled much of the province. The sultan's refusal to modernize or to address Dhofar's grievances, combined with the strain of the war, convinced many that the regime could not survive. Oil had begun to flow in commercial quantities from 1967, yet little of the new wealth reached the population, sharpening the sense that Said bin Taimur's rule had become an obstacle to the country's survival.

events.title: Zanzibar separated from Oman
events.summary: Following an arbitration award, Zanzibar was formally separated from Muscat and Oman as an independent sultanate, severing the African empire and its revenues for good.

events.title: Accession of Sultan Said bin Taimur
events.summary: Said bin Taimur came to the throne and ruled an increasingly isolated and undeveloped Oman, leaning on British support to hold the country together.

events.title: Outbreak of the Dhofar rebellion
events.summary: An uprising began in the neglected southern province of Dhofar against Said bin Taimur's rule, growing into a Marxist-led insurgency that would dominate Omani affairs for a decade.

events.title: Oil exports begin
events.summary: Oman began exporting oil in commercial quantities, the start of the revenue that would later finance the country's modern transformation.

figures.name: Said bin Taimur
figures.blurb: Austere and isolationist ruler who kept Oman undeveloped and dependent on Britain, and whose refusal to modernize amid the Dhofar war led to his overthrow in 1970.

pullquote.text: By 1970 Oman had oil in the ground but no schools to speak of, a sultan who feared change, and a rebellion that held most of the south.

eras.title: The Renaissance of Qaboos and Modern Oman
eras.standfirst: The 1970 accession of Sultan Qaboos ended Oman's isolation and launched a fifty-year renaissance; his successor, Sultan Haitham, now steers the country into a post-oil future.
body: On 23 July 1970 Qaboos bin Said, the British-educated son of Said bin Taimur, deposed his father in a palace coup and took the throne, sending the old sultan into exile. Making the defeat of the Dhofar rebellion his first priority, Qaboos combined military action, supported by Britain and Iran, with amnesty and development for the south. By 1975 the insurgency had been overcome, and the new sultan turned the country's oil wealth to a sweeping programme of modernization that Omanis came to call the Renaissance, or Nahda.

Under Qaboos, Oman was transformed almost beyond recognition: roads, schools, hospitals, ports and communications spread across a country that had had almost none, and the sultanate ended its long isolation by joining the United Nations and the Arab League and opening relations with its neighbours. In 1996 Qaboos promulgated the Basic Law, Oman's first written constitution, defining the institutions of the state. Abroad, he forged a distinctive foreign policy of neutrality and quiet mediation, keeping channels open to rivals across the region and making Oman a trusted go-between.

When Qaboos died on 10 January 2020 after nearly half a century in power, the succession passed smoothly to his cousin Haitham bin Tariq, named by a sealed letter and sworn in the next day. Sultan Haitham has launched Oman Vision 2040 and a new Basic Statute in 2021, establishing for the first time a formal crown prince and a primogeniture succession, while pressing economic diversification to reduce the country's reliance on oil. As of 2026 Oman remains a stable, neutral sultanate navigating the transition from the long Qaboos era toward a more diversified future.

events.title: Qaboos accedes in a palace coup
events.summary: Qaboos bin Said deposed his father Said bin Taimur and became sultan, ending Oman's isolation and beginning the modernization drive known as the Renaissance.

events.title: Defeat of the Dhofar rebellion
events.summary: With British and Iranian support and a policy of amnesty and development, Qaboos's forces overcame the Dhofar insurgency, securing the unity of the country.

events.title: Promulgation of the Basic Law
events.summary: Sultan Qaboos issued the Basic Law, Oman's first written constitution, defining the organs and guiding principles of the state.

events.title: Death of Qaboos and accession of Haitham
events.summary: Sultan Qaboos died after nearly fifty years on the throne, and his cousin Haitham bin Tariq was sworn in as sultan the following day in a smooth succession.

events.title: New Basic Statute and Vision 2040
events.summary: Sultan Haitham issued a new Basic Statute establishing a formal crown prince and primogeniture succession, advancing Oman Vision 2040 and economic diversification.

figures.name: Qaboos bin Said
figures.blurb: Architect of modern Oman, who overthrew his father in 1970, ended the country's isolation, defeated the Dhofar rebellion and led a half-century renaissance.

figures.name: Haitham bin Tariq
figures.blurb: Cousin and successor of Qaboos, who acceded in January 2020 and has launched Oman Vision 2040, a new Basic Statute and a primogeniture succession.

pullquote.text: In half a century Qaboos took Oman from a country almost without schools or roads to a modern, neutral state respected far beyond its size.

figures.name: Ahmad bin Said al-Busaidi
figures.role: Founder and first imam of the Al Bu Said dynasty
figures.blurb: Governor of Suhar who expelled the Persian invaders and was elected imam around 1744-1749, reuniting Oman and founding the dynasty that still rules the country today.

figures.name: Said bin Sultan
figures.role: Sultan of Muscat, Oman and Zanzibar
figures.blurb: Ruler from 1806 who built Oman's Indian Ocean empire, made Zanzibar the commercial capital of East Africa, and transferred his capital there in 1840.

figures.name: Qaboos bin Said
figures.blurb: Architect of modern Oman, who deposed his father in 1970, ended the country's isolation, defeated the Dhofar rebellion and led a half-century renaissance until his death.
